learning_objectives: Explain the difference between additive (RGB) and subtractive (CMYK) color models and when each applies
Apply color harmony principles (complementary, analogous, triadic, split-complementary) to digital compositions
Use HSB/HSL color space to make precise, intentional adjustments to hue, saturation, and brightness
Construct a mood-appropriate color palette for a game concept using systematic methods
readings: Color and Light by James Gurney, Ch. 1-4
Interaction of Color by Josef Albers, Exercises 1-5
Understanding Color Theory for Game Design (Gamasutra article by Herman Tulleken)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Welcome to Digital Art Fundamentals. This course teaches the visual principles and technical skills you need to create game art, UI elements, and digital illustrations. Whether you aspire to be a character artist, environment artist, UI designer, or art director, the foundations we cover this semester apply to every visual discipline in game development.

We begin with the foundation that underpins every visual decision: color. Color is not decoration; it is communication. A warm palette signals safety, comfort, and energy. A cool palette evokes distance, mystery, and melancholy. High saturation creates excitement and intensity. Low saturation creates subtlety and atmosphere. Every color choice in a game communicates something to the player, whether the designer intends it or not. Learning to control that communication is the first step toward visual literacy.

Color operates in two fundamentally different systems. Additive color (RGB) is how screens work: red, green, and blue light combine to produce white. This is the system you will work in for all game art. Subtractive color (CMYK) is how print works: cyan, magenta, yellow, and key (black) inks combine to absorb light. Understanding both systems prevents costly mistakes when moving between digital and physical media.
key_concepts: Additive color (RGB): red, green, blue light combining toward white; the color model of screens and game displays
Subtractive color (CMYK): cyan, magenta, yellow, key inks combining toward black; the color model of printed materials
The color wheel: primary, secondary, and tertiary colors arranged by hue relationship; the foundation of all harmony systems
Color harmonies: complementary (opposite), analogous (adjacent), triadic (equidistant), split-complementary (one color plus the two adjacent to its complement)
HSB/HSL color space: Hue (the color on the wheel, 0-360 degrees), Saturation (intensity from gray to pure color), Brightness/Lightness (from dark to light)
Color temperature: warm (red/orange/yellow) vs. cool (blue/green/violet) and the emotional associations of each
Simultaneous contrast: colors appear different depending on surrounding colors; a gray square looks blue next to orange and orange next to blue
Value: the lightness or darkness of a color independent of hue; the single most important property for readability
Color palettes for games: typically 5-8 core colors with defined roles (background, foreground, interactive elements, danger, reward)
Accessibility in color: designing for colorblind players (8% of men) by ensuring sufficient value contrast and using redundant coding (shape + color)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete game environment color palette in Adobe Color CC, starting from a mood reference image, then applying it to a simple scene composition in Photoshop to test readability and emotional tone.
setup_requirements: Adobe Photoshop, Krita, or GIMP (any raster editor with color tools)
Graphics tablet recommended but not required for this session
Color wheel reference chart (provided)

review.value: Screens use RGB (additive, light-based, white is all colors combined). Print uses CMYK (subtractive, ink-based, black is all colors combined). All game art is RGB.
Systematic relationships on the color wheel that produce visually pleasing and communicative combinations. Critical for readable game UI and cohesive environment art.
The lightness/darkness of a color is more important than its hue for readability. A composition that works in grayscale will work in color; the reverse is not guaranteed.
Colors change appearance based on their surroundings. Always test your palette in context, not in isolation, because a color that looks right in a swatch may look wrong in the scene.

learning_objectives: Apply the rule of thirds, golden ratio, and leading lines to game art compositions
Create visual hierarchy using size, contrast, color, and position to guide the viewer's eye
Analyze how composition guides attention in game screenshots and UI layouts
Use thumbnail sketching to explore multiple compositions before committing to a final layout
readings: Framed Ink by Marcos Mateu-Mestre, Ch. 1-3
Design Basics Index by Jim Krause, Composition section
Level Design for Games: Creating Compelling Game Experiences, Ch. 7 (Visual Composition)

lecture_notes.introduction:
A technically skilled artist who cannot compose an image leaves the viewer lost. Composition is the invisible architecture that directs attention, communicates importance, and creates emotional impact. In game art, composition is not optional; it is functional. The player needs to know where to look, what is important, and what to do, all within a fraction of a second.

The rule of thirds is the most basic composition tool: divide the canvas into a 3x3 grid and place focal points at the intersections. This simple guideline produces more dynamic and engaging compositions than centering every element. The golden ratio (approximately 1.618:1) is a related but more refined proportion found throughout art and nature. Leading lines are paths within the image that guide the viewer's eye toward the focal point, whether they are literal paths, edges of buildings, directional lighting, or character gaze direction.

Visual hierarchy determines the order in which the viewer processes information. The largest, highest-contrast, most saturated, and most centrally placed element is perceived first. In game UI, this means the most critical information (health, objective marker, immediate threat) must dominate the visual hierarchy, while less critical information (minimap, ability cooldowns, inventory shortcuts) recedes. Mastering hierarchy is the difference between a UI that players read instantly and one they ignore or misread under pressure.
key_concepts: Rule of thirds: dividing the canvas into a 3x3 grid and placing focal points at intersections for dynamic, engaging composition
Golden ratio / golden spiral: a proportion (~1.618:1) found in art and nature that produces naturally pleasing spatial relationships
Leading lines: elements within the image (roads, edges, gaze direction, light beams) that guide the viewer's eye toward the focal point
Visual hierarchy: arranging elements so the most important content is perceived first, using size, contrast, color saturation, and position
Negative space: intentionally empty areas that prevent clutter, create focus, and allow the viewer's eye to rest between elements
Focal point: the single most important element in a composition; every other element should support attention toward it
Tangent avoidance: preventing edges of different elements from touching or nearly touching, which creates visual confusion about depth
Thumbnail sketching: small (2-3 inch) rough sketches to explore multiple compositions quickly before committing to a full-size piece
Dynamic vs. static composition: diagonal lines and asymmetry create energy and movement; horizontal lines and symmetry create calm and stability
Composition in game contexts: HUD overlays must not obscure gameplay; UI panels must guide the eye through information in priority order
lecture_notes.demonstration: Overlaying composition grids on game screenshots from Journey, Hollow Knight, and Ori and the Blind Forest to show rule-of-thirds and leading-line usage, followed by a thumbnail sketching exercise for a hypothetical game title screen.
setup_requirements: Image editing software with grid overlay capability
Sketchbook or digital drawing tool for thumbnail exercises
Collection of game screenshots for analysis (provided)

review.value: The deliberate arrangement of elements by importance using size, contrast, color, and position. The viewer processes the highest-hierarchy element first.
Intentionally empty areas that prevent visual clutter and create breathing room. In game UI, negative space separates information groups and reduces cognitive load.
Small, fast compositional studies that explore multiple layouts before investing time in a final piece. Three minutes per thumbnail, ten thumbnails before picking one.
Any visual element that creates a directional path guiding the eye. In games, leading lines help players identify paths, objectives, and important objects without explicit markers.

learning_objectives: Configure brush settings for line work, shading, and texture in a raster editor with pressure sensitivity
Use layers, blend modes, and masks effectively for non-destructive workflow
Apply a structured digital painting workflow: thumbnail, rough sketch, clean line art, flat color, shading, detail
Create and organize custom brush presets for different art tasks
readings: Digital Painting Techniques by 3DTotal Publishing, Ch. 1-2
Beginner's Guide to Digital Painting in Photoshop by 3DTotal, Introduction and Ch. 1
Ctrl+Paint (online) - Digital Painting 101 video series

lecture_notes.introduction:
Knowing your tools is as important as knowing your theory. A painter who does not understand their brushes wastes time fighting the medium instead of expressing ideas. The same applies to digital art: mastering your software and hardware transforms them from obstacles into extensions of your creative intent.

Today we cover the practical skills of digital drawing, from brush configuration to a professional painting workflow. The key concept is non-destructive editing: techniques that let you make changes without permanently altering your original work. Layers separate different elements so you can modify one without affecting others. Masks hide or reveal areas without erasing pixels. Adjustment layers change color and value without touching the source image. Smart objects preserve original resolution through transformations. These techniques are not just convenience features; they are essential for professional work where clients request changes and deadlines require quick revisions.

The structured workflow we will learn today (thumbnail, rough, lines, flats, shading, detail) is not a rigid prescription but a reliable default process that prevents the most common beginner mistakes: jumping to detail before establishing the composition, adding color before confirming values, and polishing areas before the whole image works as a unit. You will develop your own workflow over time, but this one provides a solid starting point.
key_concepts: Raster vs. vector: pixels (resolution-dependent, good for painting and detail) versus mathematical paths (resolution-independent, good for clean shapes and scaling)
Brush dynamics: size, opacity, flow, pressure sensitivity curves, tilt response, and the difference between opacity and flow
Custom brush creation: building brushes from texture stamps, adjusting scatter, spacing, and angle jitter for natural-looking strokes
Layer management: naming conventions, groups/folders, clipping masks, and the discipline of separating line art, colors, and effects onto different layers
Blend modes: Multiply (darkens, great for shadows), Screen (lightens, great for highlights), Overlay (increases contrast), Color (changes hue/saturation without affecting value)
Non-destructive editing: adjustment layers for color/value changes, layer masks for selective visibility, smart objects for scalable embedded content
The structured workflow: (1) thumbnail compositions, (2) rough sketch for proportions, (3) clean line art, (4) flat colors on separate layers, (5) lighting and shadow pass, (6) detail and polish pass
Pressure sensitivity calibration: adjusting tablet pressure curves so light strokes produce thin/transparent lines and heavy strokes produce thick/opaque lines
Canvas management: working at 2x final resolution for quality, flipping the canvas horizontally to catch proportion errors, and zooming out regularly to check the whole image
File organization: naming layers descriptively, using color-coded groups, and saving working files in native format (PSD/KRA) separately from export formats (PNG)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live digital painting of a game character from rough sketch to finished illustration using the structured workflow, narrating each decision and technique in real time.
setup_requirements: Graphics tablet with pressure sensitivity (Wacom, Huion, or XP-Pen)
Adobe Photoshop, Krita, or Clip Studio Paint installed and configured
Custom brush pack (provided on course page)

review.value: Mathematical operations controlling how layer pixels interact. Multiply darkens (shadows), Screen lightens (highlights), Overlay boosts contrast. These three handle 80% of blend mode needs.
Using masks, adjustment layers, and smart objects to make changes without permanently altering pixel data. Essential for professional workflows where revisions are expected.
Calibrating tablet pressure so strokes respond naturally to hand pressure. Light touch for sketching and thin lines; heavy pressure for bold strokes and fills.
Thumbnail, rough, lines, flats, shading, detail. Work from big to small, general to specific. Confirm each stage works before advancing to the next.

learning_objectives: Create readable pixel art sprites at common game resolutions (16x16, 32x32, 64x64)
Apply dithering, anti-aliasing, and limited-palette techniques to maximize visual quality within constraints
Animate a pixel art character using a basic 4-frame walk cycle
Evaluate pixel art quality using readability, consistency, and silhouette criteria
readings: Pixel Logic by Michael Azzi, Ch. 1-4
The Spriters Resource tutorials on animation fundamentals
Pixel Art: Where to Start (Saint11 tutorial series, online)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Pixel art is not merely nostalgia. It is a discipline of extreme economy where every pixel must earn its place. At 16x16 resolution, you have 256 total pixels to communicate a character's identity, posture, direction, and emotional state. There is no room for waste, and every pixel's color and position matters.

The constraints of pixel art are severe: tiny canvas sizes, limited color palettes, and the inability to use anti-aliasing or gradients the way high-resolution art does. But these constraints are what make pixel art a powerful teaching tool for visual communication. If you can make a character recognizable and appealing at 16x16, you understand the fundamentals of shape language, silhouette, value contrast, and color economy at a level that transfers directly to high-resolution work.

Pixel art also requires understanding techniques that are unique to the medium. Dithering simulates gradients by alternating pixels of two colors in patterns. Manual anti-aliasing (also called sel-out or selective outlining) places intermediate-color pixels at jagged edges to create smoother curves. These techniques are the equivalent of brushwork in traditional painting: the low-level craft that separates amateur work from professional quality.
key_concepts: Resolution and scale: choosing sprite size based on game resolution, screen density, and the level of detail the game requires
Silhouette readability: at small sizes, the character must be recognizable by outline alone; a distinctive silhouette is more important than internal detail
Outline techniques: full outline (dark border around entire sprite), selective outline/sel-out (colored outline matching adjacent fill), no outline (fill colors define the shape)
Dithering: alternating pixels of two colors in patterns to simulate a gradient when the palette is too limited for smooth transitions
Manual anti-aliasing: placing intermediate-color pixels at jagged staircase edges to create the illusion of smoother curves
Limited palette discipline: restricting colors to 8-16 per sprite forces intentional color choices and creates cohesive visual style
Pixel clusters and banding: avoiding unintentional patterns of same-colored pixels that create visual noise or unwanted contours
Animation at pixel scale: walk cycles (4-8 frames), idle breathing (2-4 frames), attack anticipation/swing/follow-through
Sub-pixel animation: shifting colors within a static outline to create the illusion of movement smaller than one pixel
Consistency rules: maintaining the same light source direction, outline weight, palette, and proportions across all sprites in a game
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a 32x32 character sprite in Aseprite from scratch, including silhouette design, color blocking, shading, and anti-aliasing, followed by animating a 4-frame walk cycle.
setup_requirements: Aseprite, Piskel (free web-based), or GraphicsGale for pixel editing
Grid-based reference sheet for common sprite proportions (provided)
Graphics tablet or mouse (both work well for pixel art)

review.value: Alternating pixels of two colors in a pattern to simulate a gradient. Essential when working with limited palettes. Checkerboard is the most common pattern; other patterns (vertical, horizontal, clustered) create different texture effects.
Using colored outlines that match adjacent fill colors instead of uniform black. Produces a softer, more integrated look at small resolutions and reduces the harsh 'paper doll' effect.
Fill the sprite with solid black. If you can still identify the character, the silhouette works. At small resolutions, silhouette is the primary communication tool.
Four frames minimum: contact (foot forward), passing (legs crossing), contact (other foot forward), passing (legs crossing back). Head bobs slightly on passing frames.

learning_objectives: Create scalable vector game assets using Bezier curves, anchor points, and boolean operations
Design clean, consistent icon sets using a grid system and shared design language
Export vector assets in appropriate formats for different game engines (SVG, PDF, PNG at multiple resolutions)
Apply the principles of geometric simplification to create readable icons at small sizes
readings: Vector Basic Training by Von Glitschka, Ch. 1-5
The Icon Design Guide by Jon Hicks, Ch. 1-3
Google Material Design Icon Guidelines (online documentation)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Vector graphics give you resolution-independent assets that scale to any screen size without quality loss. Where raster images become blurry when enlarged, vectors remain crisp because they are defined by mathematical relationships rather than pixel grids. This makes vectors essential for UI elements, icons, logos, and any asset that must look sharp across multiple devices and resolutions.

The foundation of vector graphics is the Bezier curve, a mathematically defined path controlled by anchor points and direction handles. Mastering the pen tool, which creates Bezier curves, is the single most important technical skill in vector work. It takes practice, but once you internalize how anchor points and handles control curve shape, you can create any form with precision and elegance.

Boolean operations (union, subtract, intersect, exclude) let you build complex shapes from simple geometric primitives. A character icon can be constructed from circles, rectangles, and triangles combined through boolean operations, producing clean, geometric shapes that are easy to modify and consistent in style. This constructive approach is faster and more consistent than drawing freeform curves for every element.
key_concepts: Bezier curves: anchor points define positions, handles define curve direction and magnitude; the mathematical foundation of all vector graphics
The pen tool: creating paths by placing anchor points and adjusting handles; practice exercise: trace the alphabet using only the pen tool
Boolean operations: union (combine shapes), subtract (cut one from another), intersect (keep only overlapping area), exclude (keep only non-overlapping areas)
Constructive geometry: building complex shapes from simple primitives (circles, rectangles, triangles) using boolean operations
Grid-based icon design: working on a consistent pixel grid (24x24, 32x32, 48x48) to ensure alignment and optical consistency across an icon set
Optical correction: adjusting shapes that are mathematically correct but look wrong (circles appear smaller than same-width squares; round shapes need to slightly overshoot the grid)
Stroke vs. fill: when to use outlined icons (lighter, more technical) vs. filled icons (bolder, better at small sizes); how to expand strokes for game export
Path simplification: reducing anchor point count while maintaining shape accuracy; fewer points produce smoother curves and smaller file sizes
Export formats: SVG for web and scalable use, PDF for print and documentation, PNG at 1x/2x/3x for game engine import at specific resolutions
Design language consistency: maintaining uniform stroke width, corner radius, proportion, and detail level across all icons in a set
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a set of 6 game UI icons (health, mana, attack, defense, inventory, settings) in Figma using a 32x32 grid system, boolean operations, and consistent stroke width, then exporting at multiple resolutions.
setup_requirements: Adobe Illustrator, Inkscape, Figma, or Affinity Designer
Grid template file (provided on course page)
Mouse or tablet (both work well for vector work)

review.value: Paths defined by anchor points and control handles. The mathematical foundation of all vector graphics. Mastering the pen tool is the essential technical skill for vector work.
Union, subtract, intersect, and exclude operations combine simple shapes into complex ones. Faster and more consistent than freeform drawing for geometric design.
Working on a consistent pixel grid ensures icons align optically, scale predictably, and maintain visual consistency across an entire set.
Shapes that are mathematically identical look different to the eye. Circles appear smaller than squares of equal width. Round shapes must slightly overshoot grid boundaries to look aligned.

learning_objectives: Apply typographic principles (hierarchy, legibility, pairing) to game UI text across multiple contexts
Select and pair fonts appropriate for different game genres and reading contexts
Configure type settings (size, leading, tracking, kerning) for optimal screen readability
Evaluate font licensing requirements for commercial game projects
readings: Thinking with Type by Ellen Lupton, Ch. 1-3
The Elements of Typographic Style by Robert Bringhurst, Ch. 2 (abridged)
Game Typography: A Brief Guide (online resource by Emma Backer)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Typography in games serves a functional purpose that goes beyond aesthetics: players read ability descriptions during combat, scan item stats while managing inventory, follow dialogue during story moments, and process tutorial text while learning new mechanics. Every piece of text competes for the player's limited attention, often under time pressure. If the typography fails, the player fails.

The fundamental principle of game typography is legibility under adverse conditions. Game text must be readable at distance (console games viewed from a couch), at small sizes (mobile screens), in motion (HUD elements during gameplay), and over varied backgrounds (transparent UI over changing game scenes). This is a much harder problem than typography for print or web, where the designer controls the background and the reading distance.

Font pairing is the art of selecting complementary typefaces that work together without competing. A display font provides personality and sets the tone for titles and headers. A body font provides clarity and readability for long text passages. A monospace font provides alignment and technical precision for numerical data. Selecting the right combination, and knowing when to use each, is a design decision that affects every screen in the game.
key_concepts: Serif vs. sans-serif: serifs (small strokes at letter ends) aid reading in print but can reduce legibility at small screen sizes; sans-serif fonts are generally preferred for game UI
X-height: the height of lowercase letters; higher x-height improves readability at small sizes because more of each letter's shape is visible
Kerning: adjusting space between specific letter pairs for optical evenness; critical for titles and logos where spacing errors are visible
Leading (line spacing): the vertical distance between baselines; too tight causes lines to blend, too loose breaks reading flow; 120-150% of font size is typical
Tracking (letter spacing): uniform spacing adjustment across entire text blocks; slight positive tracking improves readability of all-caps text
Font pairing: display font for titles (expressive, personality), body font for content (legible, neutral), monospace font for data (aligned, technical)
Minimum readable sizes: body text at minimum 16px on mobile, 24px on console (viewed from 6-10 feet), 12-14px on PC (viewed from 2-3 feet)
Text contrast: WCAG guidelines require 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text and 3:1 for large text; use contrast checkers for verification
Dynamic text challenges: localization (German text is ~30% longer than English), player-created names, variable stat numbers, and text that wraps at different screen widths
Font licensing: free-for-commercial-use (Google Fonts, OFL), paid licenses (Adobe Fonts, MyFonts), and the legal requirement to verify licensing before shipping
lecture_notes.demonstration: Font selection and pairing exercise for a fantasy RPG game menu: choosing a display font for the title, a body font for descriptions, and a monospace font for stat tables, then building a menu mockup in Figma with proper hierarchy, sizing, and spacing.
setup_requirements: Access to Google Fonts or similar free font library
Figma, Adobe XD, or equivalent UI design tool
WCAG contrast checker (online tool)

review.value: Using size, weight, color, and spacing to create a clear reading order. Players should scan game UI and instantly know what is most important without reading every word.
Display font for titles (personality), body font for content (legibility), monospace for data (alignment). Pairs should contrast in style but share visual weight and proportion.
16px minimum on mobile, 24px on console, 12px on PC. These are legibility floors, not targets. When in doubt, go larger.
Design text areas to accommodate 30-40% longer strings for localization. German, Finnish, and Russian text is significantly longer than English for the same content.

class_schedule.topic: UI and HUD Design for Games
learning_objectives: Design a functional game UI layout that follows platform conventions and supports gameplay
Apply information architecture principles to organize game data into logical, scannable groups
Create HUD elements that communicate critical gameplay information without obstructing the view
Design responsive UI that adapts to different screen sizes and aspect ratios
readings: Game UI Design by Don L. Daglow, online article series
Designing the User Experience of Game Interfaces (GDC talk by Stone Librande)
UI Best Practices for PC, Console, and Mobile (Unity documentation)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Game UI is the bridge between the player and the game system. Every piece of information the player needs, their health, their objectives, their inventory, their abilities, flows through the UI. A great UI communicates this information instantly, intuitively, and unobtrusively. A poor UI forces the player to fight the interface instead of playing the game.

Game UI has two primary components. The HUD (heads-up display) overlays gameplay with real-time information: health bars, minimaps, ability cooldowns, score counters, and objective markers. The HUD must communicate critical data without obstructing the game world, which means every pixel of HUD space has a high cost. If information can be communicated through the game world itself (environmental health indicators, in-world objective markers), it should be, freeing HUD space for truly necessary elements.

Menu UI handles everything outside of gameplay: main menus, inventory screens, character sheets, settings panels, and dialogue interfaces. Menu design follows information architecture principles: organizing data into logical groups, establishing visual hierarchy within each group, and providing consistent navigation patterns so the player always knows where they are and how to get where they want to go.
key_concepts: HUD design principles: show only what the player needs right now; hide information that is not immediately relevant; use progressive disclosure
Diegetic UI: interface elements that exist within the game world (Dead Space's spine health meter, Fallout's Pip-Boy); increases immersion but limits flexibility
Non-diegetic UI: traditional overlay elements (health bars, minimaps, ability icons); less immersive but more flexible and readable
Platform conventions: mobile touch targets (minimum 44x44 points), console safe zones (10% margin from edges), PC flexibility (resizable windows, mouse precision)
Information architecture: grouping related information, establishing hierarchy within groups, and providing consistent navigation patterns
Progressive disclosure: showing basic information by default and revealing detail on demand (hover, tap, expand); reduces initial cognitive load
Screen composition for HUD: corners for status information (health, mana, minimap), center for immediate feedback (damage numbers, notifications), bottom for ability bars
Responsive design: UI that adapts to different screen sizes and aspect ratios; using relative positioning and scalable elements rather than fixed pixel coordinates
Animation in UI: transitions that communicate state changes (health bar draining, item appearing in inventory), providing feedback without requiring the player to read text
Accessibility in UI: high-contrast mode, scalable text, screen-reader-compatible navigation, colorblind-safe indicators
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a complete game HUD for a hypothetical action-RPG in Figma: health/mana bars, minimap, ability bar, quest tracker, and notification system, with versions for PC, console, and mobile layouts.
setup_requirements: Figma, Adobe XD, or Sketch for UI layout
Platform specification reference sheets (provided: touch target sizes, safe zones, common resolutions)
Design journal for wireframe sketches

review.value: Show only essential information by default; reveal details on demand. Reduces visual clutter and cognitive load while keeping all information accessible.
Diegetic UI exists in the game world (immersive but limited). Non-diegetic UI overlays the screen (flexible but breaks immersion). Most games use both strategically.
Mobile: 44pt minimum touch targets. Console: 10% safe zone margins, readable from 6-10 feet. PC: mouse precision, flexible windowing. Design for each platform's constraints.
Every pixel of HUD space costs the player a pixel of game world visibility. Justify every HUD element by asking: does the player need this information right now? If not, hide it.

learning_objectives: Produce concept art that communicates design intent clearly to a development team
Use reference gathering, mood boards, and visual development to establish an art direction
Apply ideation techniques (silhouette exploration, random shape generation, photo-bashing) to generate diverse concepts quickly
Present concept art with annotations that explain design decisions and functional considerations
readings: The Skillful Huntsman by Khang Le, Mike Yamada & Felix Yoon (concept art process book)
Concept Art for Film, Games, and Animation by Marcos Mateu-Mestre, Ch. 1-3
How to Make Great Concept Art (FZD School video lecture by Feng Zhu)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Concept art is not illustration. Its purpose is not to be beautiful on its own; its purpose is to communicate design ideas to a team so they can build the game. A concept artist is a visual problem solver who translates abstract design requirements into concrete visual solutions. The best concept art answers questions: What does this character look like from all angles? How does this environment make the player feel? What is this creature's silhouette, scale, and color language?

The concept art process begins with research and reference gathering. No artist works from pure imagination; even the most fantastical designs are built from real-world reference combined in new ways. A dragon's wing structure borrows from bat anatomy. An alien cityscape references brutalist architecture. A magical forest draws from bioluminescent deep-sea organisms. The more reference you gather, the more specific and believable your designs become.

Ideation techniques help you generate many concepts quickly before settling on one to refine. Silhouette exploration produces dozens of distinct shapes in minutes. Random shape generation provides unexpected starting points that push designs beyond your habitual comfort zone. Photo-bashing combines photographic elements into rough compositions that establish mood and scale. Speed and volume are the priorities during ideation; refinement comes later.
key_concepts: Concept art vs. illustration: concept art communicates design to a team; illustration communicates a finished image to an audience. Different purposes, different standards.
Reference gathering: collecting real-world photos, existing art, textures, and materials that inform the visual direction; organized into mood boards by theme
Mood boards: curated collections of reference images that establish the emotional tone, color palette, material quality, and visual language of a project
Silhouette exploration: drawing 20-30 small silhouettes quickly to explore a wide range of shapes for a character, creature, or prop before adding detail
Random shape ideation: generating random shapes (ink blots, geometric combinations, 3D kitbashing) and interpreting them as design starting points
Photo-bashing: compositing photographic elements into rough concept paintings for speed; effective for environments and hard-surface design
Callout sheets: detailed annotations pointing to specific design features (material, color, scale reference, functional notes) that complement the visual
Turnarounds: front, side, and back views of a character or object at consistent proportion, providing the information a 3D modeler needs
Scale reference: including a human figure or familiar object in concept art to communicate how large or small the designed element is
Iteration over perfection: producing many rough concepts is more valuable than producing one polished one, because the best idea is rarely the first idea
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live concept art session: starting with a design brief ('ancient guardian of an underwater temple'), gathering reference in 5 minutes, generating 10 silhouette variants in 10 minutes, selecting the strongest 3, and developing one into a refined concept with annotations.
setup_requirements: Graphics tablet and raster painting software
Pinterest, PureRef, or equivalent for reference management
Timer for timed ideation exercises

review.value: Concept art answers design questions for the team. Illustration creates a finished image for an audience. In production, speed and clarity beat beauty.
Drawing many small silhouettes quickly explores a wide design space. If a character is not recognizable in silhouette, no amount of detail will save it.
Compositing photographic elements into rough concept paintings. Extremely fast for establishing mood, lighting, and material quality in environment concepts.
Labels and notes on concept art that explain material, color, function, and design intent. Concept art without annotations is incomplete because visual alone cannot communicate every decision.

learning_objectives: Design game characters using shape language to communicate personality and role
Apply anatomical proportion guidelines appropriate to different art styles (realistic, stylized, chibi)
Create a character design sheet with front, side, and expression views
Use color and costume design to reinforce character role and faction identity
readings: Creating Characters with Personality by Tom Bancroft, Ch. 1-4
Force: Dynamic Life Drawing by Mike Mattesi, Ch. 1-3
Character Design Quarterly, Issue 12 (selected articles on game character design)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Character design in games must accomplish something that character design in other media does not: the character must be readable at the size and speed the game presents them. A fighting game character viewed from 10 feet away on a TV must be recognizable by silhouette alone. A real-time strategy unit viewed at 50 pixels tall must communicate its type and faction through color and shape. An RPG character in a dialogue screen must express emotion through facial animation.

Shape language is the designer's most powerful tool for communicating character personality at a glance. Round shapes suggest friendliness, warmth, and approachability (Mario, Kirby). Angular shapes suggest aggression, danger, and precision (Pyramid Head, Genji). Square shapes suggest stability, reliability, and strength (Heavy from TF2, Reinhardt from Overwatch). Combining these shapes in different proportions creates characters that players intuitively understand before reading a single line of dialogue.

Beyond shape, color carries meaning. Warm colors read as allied and safe in most game contexts. Cool colors read as mysterious or hostile. High saturation signals importance. Desaturation signals mundanity. Faction colors create instant team identification. The character's color must work against the environments they will appear in, maintaining readability over varied backgrounds.
key_concepts: Shape language: circles (friendly, soft), triangles (dangerous, dynamic), squares (stable, strong); the psychological associations of basic shapes applied to character design
Anatomical proportions: realistic (7-8 heads tall), heroic (8-9 heads tall), stylized (4-6 heads tall), chibi (2-3 heads tall); choosing proportion based on art style and game genre
Silhouette clarity: a character must be recognizable by outline alone; distinctive headgear, weapons, posture, or body proportions differentiate characters in gameplay
Color scripting for characters: assigning colors based on role (red for danger/aggression, blue for calm/intelligence, green for nature/healing, purple for magic/mystery)
Costume design as communication: clothing, armor, and accessories tell the player about the character's role, faction, culture, economic status, and personality
Expression sheets: showing the character's face in key emotional states (neutral, happy, angry, surprised, sad) to guide animators and ensure the design supports expression
Turnaround sheets: front, 3/4, side, and back views at consistent proportion for 3D modelers or sprite artists to reference
Design consistency within a cast: characters in the same game should share a visual language (line weight, color saturation, proportion system) while being individually distinct
Readability at game scale: testing the character design at the actual size it will appear in-game; detail that is beautiful up close may be invisible at runtime
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a game character from scratch: starting with role/personality brief, exploring 8 silhouette variants, developing the strongest into a full design with shape language analysis, color selection, and a turnaround sheet.
setup_requirements: Graphics tablet and drawing software
Shape language reference sheet (provided)
Anatomy proportion guide (provided)

review.value: Circles signal friendliness, triangles signal danger, squares signal stability. The dominant shape in a character's design communicates their personality before any detail is read.
Fill the character with solid black and check if they are still identifiable. If two characters in the same game have indistinguishable silhouettes, at least one needs redesigning.
Zoom out to the actual in-game size and check readability. Beautiful detail that disappears at runtime is wasted effort. Design for the size the player actually sees.
Front, 3/4, side, and back views at consistent scale. Essential for 3D modelers and animators. Include construction lines showing proportion relationships.

learning_objectives: Design game environments that communicate mood, guide navigation, and support gameplay
Apply atmospheric perspective, value staging, and lighting to create depth in 2D scenes
Create tileable environment pieces that connect seamlessly for modular level construction
Use color temperature gradients and focal points to direct the player's eye through a scene
readings: How to Draw by Scott Robertson, Ch. 8-10 (Environment Perspective)
Color and Light by James Gurney, Ch. 10-14 (Outdoor and Indoor Lighting)
Environment Art Best Practices for Games (ArtStation Learning article series)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Environment art in games serves a dual purpose: it creates the world the player inhabits and it guides the player through that world. A forest is not just a collection of trees; it is a designed space that channels movement, reveals information, hides secrets, and communicates mood. Every element in a game environment, the lighting, the color temperature, the amount of detail, the placement of landmarks, is a design decision that affects how the player navigates and feels.

Depth is the fundamental challenge of environment art. Games are displayed on flat screens, but players need to perceive three-dimensional space to navigate effectively. Atmospheric perspective (distant objects are lighter, bluer, and less detailed) is the most powerful depth cue for outdoor environments. Value staging (separating foreground, midground, and background into distinct value ranges) ensures readability even in busy scenes. Strategic lighting creates focal points that guide the eye and establishes the emotional tone of the space.

Modular environment design is the practical reality of game production. Rather than painting every screen as a unique illustration, environment artists create tileable pieces (ground tiles, wall segments, platform elements, decorative props) that can be combined in a level editor to build varied spaces from a shared asset library. The art challenge is making modular pieces that connect seamlessly while avoiding the visual repetition that makes tiled environments look artificial.
key_concepts: Atmospheric perspective: distant objects appear lighter, lower in contrast, bluer/cooler, and less detailed; the primary depth cue in outdoor environments
Value staging: assigning distinct value ranges to foreground (darkest), midground (medium), and background (lightest) to separate visual planes
Lighting as guidance: bright areas attract the eye; dark areas recede; strategic lighting directs the player toward objectives and away from boundaries
Color temperature gradients: warm foreground to cool background creates natural depth; reversing this (cool foreground, warm background) creates unease
Tileable assets: environment pieces designed to repeat seamlessly by ensuring edges match when placed adjacent; tested by tiling a 3x3 grid
Modular design: creating a library of interchangeable pieces (floor, wall, corner, door, window, prop) that combine into varied environments
Landmark design: distinctive, visible elements placed at navigation decision points so players can orient themselves and remember locations
Environmental storytelling: using props, damage, wear, and arrangement to communicate narrative (a broken chair tells a different story than an intact one)
Scale and proportion: maintaining consistent scale between characters and environment elements; using door frames, furniture, and steps as scale reference
Painterly vs. graphic: the spectrum from realistic detailed painting to flat graphic shapes; style should match the game's overall art direction
lecture_notes.demonstration: Painting a game environment scene from value block-in to finished piece: starting with a value thumbnail, establishing atmospheric perspective, lighting the focal point, adding color, and finally detail. Then creating a tileable ground texture in 256x256 pixels.
setup_requirements: Graphics tablet and painting software
Perspective grid template (provided)
Reference images for environment type (provided)

review.value: Objects become lighter, less saturated, cooler, and less detailed with distance. The single most important technique for creating believable depth in environment art.
Separating foreground, midground, and background into distinct value ranges ensures the scene reads clearly even when viewed quickly during gameplay.
Assets must repeat seamlessly when placed edge-to-edge. Test by tiling in a 3x3 grid and checking for visible seams or repeating patterns that break immersion.
Light directs the eye. Brighten paths, doorways, and objectives. Darken boundaries and dead ends. The player follows light instinctively.

learning_objectives: Set up a complete asset pipeline from source files through export to game engine import
Create sprite sheets and texture atlases for efficient game rendering
Configure game engine import settings (compression, filtering, atlas packing) for optimal quality and performance
Organize source files and exports using consistent naming conventions and folder structures
readings: Texture Atlas and Sprite Sheet Best Practices (Unity documentation)
Asset Pipeline: From Photoshop to Unity (online tutorial by Brackeys)
Technical Art Bootcamp: The Importance of Naming Conventions (GDC talk)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Beautiful art that does not reach the game engine in the right format at the right size is useless. The asset pipeline is the complete workflow from creating art in a source tool through export, optimization, and import into the game engine with correct settings. Every step in this pipeline has technical requirements that artists must understand, or their work will look wrong, perform poorly, or fail to load.

The most critical technical concept is the difference between source files and export files. Source files (PSD, KRA, AI, SVG) preserve all layers, paths, and editability. Export files (PNG, JPG, WebP) are flattened, compressed, and optimized for the game engine. You must always keep your source files, because you will need to make changes. You must always export to the correct format, because the engine cannot read your source files.

Sprite sheets and texture atlases are the standard technique for efficient 2D game rendering. Instead of loading each sprite as a separate image file (which requires a separate GPU draw call), multiple sprites are packed into a single image with a metadata file describing where each sprite is located within the atlas. This dramatically reduces draw calls and improves rendering performance, especially on mobile devices where GPU efficiency is critical.
key_concepts: Source vs. export: source files (PSD, KRA, AI) preserve editability; export files (PNG, SVG, WebP) are optimized for engine consumption. Never discard source files.
Sprite sheets: a single image containing multiple animation frames arranged in a grid; the engine reads frames sequentially using row/column coordinates
Texture atlases: a single image containing diverse assets (icons, UI elements, props) packed tightly together; reduces GPU draw calls by batching rendering
Atlas packing tools: TexturePacker, Spine's atlas packer, Unity Sprite Atlas; tools that automatically arrange assets and generate metadata JSON/XML files

Image compression: lossy (JPG, WebP with quality < 100) reduces file size but degrades quality; lossless (PNG, WebP lossless) preserves quality but larger files. Game art typically uses PNG for sprites and compressed formats for large textures.
Texture filtering: Point/Nearest (pixel-perfect, no blurring) for pixel art, Bilinear/Trilinear (smoothed) for high-resolution art; wrong filtering makes pixel art blurry or hi-res art aliased
Power-of-two sizing: many engines require textures in power-of-two dimensions (64, 128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048) for optimal GPU memory allocation
Naming conventions: consistent patterns (character_warrior_idle_01.png, ui_icon_health.svg) that are machine-parseable and human-readable
Folder structure: organizing by asset type (characters/, environments/, ui/, effects/) with sub-folders for source and export files
Version control for art: tracking changes to source files alongside code using Git LFS or dedicated art version control systems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete asset pipeline: creating a character sprite sheet in Aseprite, exporting frames, packing them into a texture atlas using TexturePacker with JSON metadata, importing into Unity with correct filtering settings, and verifying the animation plays correctly.
setup_requirements: Aseprite or equivalent for sprite sheet export
TexturePacker (free trial) or LibGDX texture packer for atlas creation
Unity or Godot installed for import testing
File management tool (Finder/Explorer) for organizing assets

review.value: A single image containing multiple assets packed together. Reduces GPU draw calls by allowing the engine to render many sprites from one texture in a single batch.
Always keep editable source files (PSD, AI, KRA). Always export to engine-appropriate formats (PNG, SVG). Losing source files means rebuilding from scratch when changes are needed.
Point/Nearest filtering for pixel art (preserves hard edges). Bilinear for high-res art (smooths scaling). Wrong filtering is the most common visual bug in 2D game art.
Consistent, descriptive file names enable automated processing, prevent confusion, and make collaboration possible. A team that cannot find assets wastes hours every week.

learning_objectives: Apply Disney's 12 principles of animation to game character movement and UI transitions
Create a bouncing ball animation demonstrating squash and stretch, timing, and ease-in/ease-out
Animate a character attack sequence with anticipation, action, and follow-through
Evaluate animation quality using weight, readability, and emotional communication criteria
readings: The Illusion of Life by Frank Thomas & Ollie Johnston, Ch. 3 (The 12 Principles)
The Animator's Survival Kit by Richard Williams, Ch. 1-5
Animation for Games vs. Film: Key Differences (Gamasutra article)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Animation is what brings game art to life. A static character sprite becomes a living entity when it breathes, moves, reacts, and emotes. Disney's 12 principles of animation, developed in the 1930s, remain the foundation of all animation, including game animation. Understanding these principles transforms your animations from mechanical movements into performances that communicate weight, emotion, and intention.

Game animation has unique constraints that film animation does not. Game animations must loop seamlessly (idle, walk, run cycles). They must respond to player input with minimal delay, so anticipation frames must be short or cancelable. They must blend between states (walking to running, running to attacking, attacking to idle) without jarring transitions. And they must read clearly at the speed and size the game presents them, which may be much smaller and faster than the animator's working view.

Of the 12 principles, three are most critical for game animation. Squash and stretch communicates weight and flexibility; a bouncing ball that squashes on impact feels heavy, one that does not feels like a floating circle. Anticipation telegraphs upcoming actions, giving the player time to react; an enemy that winds up before attacking is fair, one that attacks without warning is frustrating. Follow-through completes the action and makes it feel committed; a sword swing that continues past the target feels powerful, one that stops at contact feels weightless.
key_concepts: Squash and stretch: deforming shapes to show impact, weight, and flexibility; the volume should remain constant (stretching makes it thinner, squashing makes it wider)
Anticipation: a preparatory movement before the main action (a crouch before a jump, a wind-up before a throw); telegraphs intent to the viewer/player
Follow-through and overlapping action: elements continue moving after the main action stops; hair, capes, and weapons follow the body's motion with delay
Staging: presenting an action clearly so the audience understands what is happening; in games, animations must read from the camera's perspective at runtime
Slow in and slow out (ease-in/ease-out): objects accelerate and decelerate rather than moving at constant speed; creates natural, weighted movement
Arcs: natural movement follows curved paths, not straight lines; joints rotate, limbs swing in arcs, projectiles follow parabolic trajectories
Timing and spacing: the number of frames and the distance between positions determine speed, weight, and feel; fewer frames = faster/lighter, more frames = slower/heavier
Secondary action: supporting movements that reinforce the main action (breathing during idle, cloth fluttering during running, hair bouncing during jumping)
Exaggeration: pushing movements beyond physical realism for clarity and impact; game animations are typically more exaggerated than realistic film animation
Game-specific constraints: looping seamlessly, blending between states, responding to input, reading at game scale, and maintaining consistent timing across frame rates
lecture_notes.demonstration: Animating a bouncing ball in Aseprite demonstrating squash/stretch, timing, and ease-in/ease-out, then animating a character attack with anticipation (wind-up), action (swing), and follow-through (recovery).
setup_requirements: Aseprite, Clip Studio Paint, or any animation-capable software
Graphics tablet for frame-by-frame drawing
Animation principles reference sheet (provided)

review.value: Deforming shapes on impact and during movement to communicate weight and flexibility. Volume stays constant: what stretches in one dimension compresses in the perpendicular dimension.
A preparatory movement before the main action. In games, anticipation frames telegraph enemy attacks (giving players time to react) and make player actions feel intentional.
Fewer frames between positions = faster, lighter movement. More frames = slower, heavier movement. Timing communicates weight, urgency, and emotional state.
Must loop seamlessly, blend between states, respond to input instantly, and read at runtime scale. These constraints shape every animation decision in game production.

class_schedule.topic: Sprite Sheets and Game Animation Production
learning_objectives: Produce a complete character sprite sheet with idle, walk, run, attack, and hit animations
Organize animation frames in engine-compatible sprite sheet formats with consistent spacing
Configure animation speed, looping, and transition settings in a game engine
Apply animation state machine concepts to manage character animation states and transitions
readings: 2D Animation in Unity (official Unity tutorial series)
Sprite Animation Best Practices for Indie Games (Gamasutra article)
The Complete Guide to Sprite Sheet Animation (Saint11 tutorial)

lecture_notes.introduction:
A single animation is a technical exercise. A complete character sprite sheet is a production deliverable. Today we bridge the gap between learning animation principles and producing game-ready animation assets that integrate into an engine and play correctly during gameplay.

A typical 2D character in an action game needs at minimum: idle (4-8 frames, looping), walk (6-8 frames, looping), run (6-8 frames, looping), jump (3-5 frames, one-shot), attack (4-8 frames, one-shot or combo), hurt/hit (2-4 frames, one-shot), and death (4-8 frames, one-shot). That is roughly 30-50 frames for a single character with basic functionality. Each frame must be consistent in size, registration point, and style, or the character will jitter, shift, and break immersion during gameplay.

Animation state machines manage which animation plays at any given moment and how transitions between animations occur. The character starts in idle. When the player presses a movement key, the state machine transitions from idle to walk (or run). When the player presses attack, the state machine transitions from the current movement state to the attack animation, then returns to the previous state when the attack completes. Understanding state machines helps animators create frames that blend smoothly between states, because they know which states connect to which.
key_concepts: Complete animation set: idle, walk, run, jump, fall, attack, hurt, death; the minimum for a functional action game character
Frame consistency: every frame in a sprite sheet must share the same canvas size, registration point (pivot), and art style; inconsistency causes visual jitter
Registration point: the pixel coordinate that remains fixed when frames change; typically the character's feet for ground-based characters, center for flying characters
Sprite sheet layout: frames arranged in rows (one row per animation) or sequentially (all frames left to right, top to bottom); accompanied by metadata defining frame regions
Animation speed: frames per second (FPS) determines playback speed; 8 FPS feels retro/chunky, 12 FPS is smooth, 24 FPS is film-quality; game FPS is independent of animation FPS
Looping vs. one-shot: looping animations (idle, walk, run) repeat indefinitely; one-shot animations (attack, death) play once and return to a resting state
Animation state machines: directed graphs where nodes are animation states and edges are transitions triggered by game events (input, collision, timer)
Transition blending: the first and last frames of connected animations should be visually compatible to prevent jarring pops when transitioning between states
Hit frames and gameplay timing: the exact frame when an attack deals damage must be communicated to the programmer; animation timing drives gameplay feel
Optimization: minimizing frame count while maintaining quality; sharing frames between animations where possible (last walk frame = first idle frame)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Producing a complete 6-animation sprite sheet for a character in Aseprite, exporting it as a packed sprite sheet with JSON metadata, importing into Unity, configuring the animation controller state machine, and playing back all animations with transitions.
setup_requirements: Aseprite with sprite sheet export configured
Unity or Godot for engine integration testing
Character sprite from previous weeks (or provided template)
Animation state machine reference diagram (provided)

review.value: Every frame must share the same pivot point. If the registration point shifts between frames, the character visually jumps during animation playback.
A graph defining which animations can transition to which others and under what conditions. The foundation of character animation management in game engines.
The specific frame where an attack deals damage must be identified and communicated to programmers. Animation timing directly determines how responsive and fair combat feels.
More frames = smoother but more art production and more memory. Find the minimum frame count that reads well at game speed. Eight frames is often enough for a walk cycle.

learning_objectives: Create a comprehensive art style guide that establishes visual rules for an entire game project
Define color palettes, proportion systems, line weight standards, and rendering approaches in a style guide
Evaluate visual consistency across multiple asset types using style guide criteria
Present an art direction vision with mood boards, reference images, and sample assets
readings: The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 19 (Look and Feel)
Creating a Game Art Bible (Riot Games art team presentation, GDC)
Art Direction Fundamentals for Games (online course excerpt by Trent Kaniuga)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Art direction is the discipline of defining and maintaining a consistent visual identity across every asset in a game. A game with inconsistent art, characters drawn in different proportions, environments with clashing color palettes, UI that looks like it belongs to a different game, undermines player immersion and communicates unprofessionalism, even if each individual asset is well-crafted.

The style guide (also called an art bible) is the central document that establishes and enforces visual consistency. It defines the rules that every artist on the project follows: the color palette with specific hex codes, the proportion system for characters, the line weight and rendering approach, the texture resolution and format standards, the naming conventions for files, and reference images showing the target aesthetic. A good style guide is specific enough that a new artist joining the project can start producing on-brand work within a day.

Creating a style guide requires you to make explicit decisions about every visual dimension of the game. What is the dominant mood? Is the palette warm or cool? High saturation or muted? Are outlines used? How thick? What is the rendering style: flat color, soft shading, hard cel-shading, painterly? How detailed are assets at their display resolution? These decisions interact, so changing one often requires adjusting others to maintain coherence. The style guide documents these decisions and their interdependencies.
key_concepts: Art bible / style guide: a living reference document defining all visual rules, standards, and references for a game project
Color palette definition: primary, secondary, accent, and neutral colors with hex codes, usage rules (which color for what purpose), and do/don't examples
Proportion system: the standard head-to-body ratio, limb proportions, and scale relationships that all characters in the game follow
Line weight standards: the thickness, tapering, and color of outlines; whether outlines vary by distance (thicker for foreground, thinner for background)
Rendering approach: flat fill, soft gradient shading, hard cel-shading, painterly, or photo-realistic; the approach must be achievable consistently across the team
Texture and resolution standards: pixel density per unit of game space, maximum texture size, required formats, compression settings
Mood boards and reference images: curated visual examples that communicate the target aesthetic more effectively than text descriptions
Do and don't examples: side-by-side comparisons showing correct and incorrect application of style rules; the most useful part of any style guide
Consistency testing: placing assets from different categories (characters, environments, UI, effects) side by side to verify they look like they belong in the same game
Style guide maintenance: updating the guide as the art evolves during production, ensuring it remains accurate and useful rather than becoming an obsolete artifact
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a style guide from scratch for a hypothetical game: defining mood (via mood board), selecting color palette, establishing proportion system, documenting line weight and rendering approach, creating do/don't examples, and testing consistency by placing a character, environment tile, and UI element side by side.
setup_requirements: Design tool (Figma, InDesign, or Google Docs) for style guide layout
Reference images and mood board materials
Sample assets from previous weeks for consistency testing

review.value: Every asset in a game must look like it belongs in the same world. The style guide ensures this by establishing explicit rules that all artists follow.
Side-by-side comparisons of correct and incorrect style application. More effective than text rules because artists can see exactly what is expected.
A style guide that is never updated becomes inaccurate and ignored. The art direction evolves during production, and the guide must evolve with it.
Place a character, environment piece, and UI element side by side. If they look like they belong in the same game, the art direction is working.

learning_objectives: Present a curated body of work demonstrating range, skill, and growth across the semester
Receive and integrate peer and instructor critique constructively
Articulate the design decisions behind each portfolio piece with clarity and confidence
Identify personal strengths and areas for continued development in game art
readings: Review all course materials and your art from the semester
Portfolio Best Practices for Game Artists (ArtStation blog, selected articles)
How I Got Hired as a Game Artist (GDC Junior Developer roundtable notes)

lecture_notes.introduction:
This final session is a portfolio review and critique. Each of you will present your strongest work from the semester, curated into a cohesive portfolio that demonstrates your skills, range, and growth as a game artist. This is not about showing everything you made; it is about selecting the pieces that best represent your abilities and presenting them with confidence.

A game art portfolio should demonstrate both technical skill and design thinking. An employer looking at your portfolio wants to see that you can create assets that are technically correct, visually appealing, and functionally appropriate for a game. They also want to see that you understand why you made the choices you did, not just that you can execute. Can you articulate why you chose that color palette? Why the character has those proportions? Why the UI is laid out that way? Design thinking is what separates an artist from a technician.

After presentations, we will conduct a course retrospective. We have covered color theory, composition, digital painting, pixel art, vector graphics, typography, UI design, concept art, character design, environment art, asset pipelines, animation principles, sprite production, and art direction. That is a complete foundation for any specialization in game art. The question now is: which direction will you go? Your portfolio tells that story.
key_concepts: Portfolio curation: selecting 8-12 strongest pieces that demonstrate range across disciplines (character, environment, UI, animation) while maintaining quality throughout
Quality over quantity: five excellent pieces beat twenty mediocre ones; every piece in the portfolio should represent your best work
Process documentation: showing thumbnails, iterations, and final results demonstrates design thinking; employers value seeing how you work, not just the outcome
Presentation format: clean, simple layout (ArtStation, personal website, or PDF); no distracting templates; the art is the focus
Critique protocol: specific observations (not vague praise), actionable suggestions, honest identification of strengths and weaknesses
Growth evidence: including early and late pieces from the semester shows improvement trajectory; employers value growth potential alongside current skill

Specialization vs. generalization: game art careers can be specialized (character artist, environment artist, UI designer) or generalized (indie developer doing everything); portfolio should signal which path you are pursuing
Artist statement: a brief written description of your interests, strengths, and artistic goals; provides context for the portfolio
Next steps: continued practice, game jam participation, online communities (ArtStation, Polycount, Pixel Joint), advanced courses, and internship applications
lecture_notes.demonstration: Student portfolio presentations (8-10 minutes each) followed by 5 minutes of peer and instructor critique per presentation.
setup_requirements: Curated portfolio (8-12 pieces) in presentation format (slides, website, or printed)
Process documentation for at least 3 pieces showing thumbnails, iterations, and final
Brief artist statement (100-200 words)

review.value: Your portfolio is only as strong as its weakest piece. Remove anything that does not represent your best work. Five excellent pieces outperform twenty mediocre ones.
Including thumbnails, iterations, and decision documentation demonstrates design thinking. Employers want to see how you solve problems, not just the finished product.
Be ready to explain why you made every visual choice. 'I chose cool colors because...' not 'I just liked how it looked.' Design reasoning elevates craft into art direction.
Showing where you started and where you are demonstrates the ability to learn and improve, which is the most valuable trait an employer can find in a junior artist.

quizzes.topic: Color Theory, Composition, and Digital Drawing Fundamentals

questions.question: A game designer asks you to create a color palette for a horror game set in an abandoned hospital. Describe which color harmony you would use, which color temperature would dominate, and explain how you would use saturation strategically to reinforce the game's mood while maintaining gameplay readability.
questions.answer: An analogous harmony centered on cool blue-greens and desaturated teals would establish an eerie, clinical atmosphere. Cool temperature dominates because it evokes isolation, sterility, and unease. Saturation should be kept very low across the environment (washed-out, institutional colors) with one or two highly saturated accent colors (vivid red for blood, danger, or interactive objects) to create visual shock and draw attention to critical gameplay elements. The contrast between the desaturated world and the rare saturated accent maximizes both mood and readability: the player instantly sees what matters because it is the only saturated element in an otherwise muted scene.

rubric.key: Identifies appropriate color harmony with reasoning connected to the horror genre
Explains color temperature choice and its emotional effect on the player
Describes saturation strategy that serves both mood reinforcement and gameplay readability

questions.question: Explain the difference between a raster image and a vector image. For each, give one game art use case where that format is the better choice and explain why.
questions.answer: A raster image is composed of a grid of pixels with a fixed resolution; it loses quality when scaled up. A vector image is defined by mathematical paths (Bezier curves) and scales to any resolution without quality loss. Raster is better for detailed character illustrations and environment paintings where subtle gradients, textures, and photographic-quality detail are needed (e.g., painted backgrounds in Hollow Knight). Vector is better for UI elements like icons, buttons, and logos that must render crisply across multiple screen resolutions and device sizes (e.g., a mobile game's menu icons that must look sharp on both phones and tablets).

rubric.key: Correct definitions of raster and vector with the key technical distinction (pixels vs. math paths)
Appropriate raster use case with reasoning about why raster is specifically better for that case
Appropriate vector use case with reasoning about why vector is specifically better for that case

questions.question: Explain what 'value' means in the context of color theory and why it is considered the most important property for readability. Then describe the 'squint test' and how an artist uses it.
questions.answer: Value is the lightness or darkness of a color, independent of hue and saturation. It is the most important property for readability because the human visual system processes value contrast before color. A composition that reads clearly in grayscale (high value contrast between elements) will read clearly in color, but a composition that relies on hue differences alone (same value, different colors) becomes unreadable when viewed in grayscale, in poor lighting, or by colorblind players. The squint test: the artist squints their eyes until the image becomes blurry, eliminating detail and reducing the image to large value shapes. If the major elements (focal point, foreground, background) are still distinguishable while squinting, the value structure is strong. If everything blurs into a uniform gray, the values need more separation.

rubric.key: Correct definition of value and its independence from hue
Explains why value is more important than hue for readability with specific reasoning
Accurately describes the squint test and what a passing/failing result looks like

quizzes.topic: Pixel Art, Vector Graphics, and Typography

questions.question: You are creating a 16x16 pixel art character for a top-down RPG. At this resolution, you have 256 total pixels. Explain two specific techniques you would use to make the character readable and recognizable despite the extreme size constraint.
questions.answer: Technique 1: High-contrast silhouette. At 16x16, internal detail is nearly impossible to read, so the character must be recognizable by outline alone. Using a dark outline against lighter fill, with the silhouette showing a distinctive feature (a hat, a weapon, or a cape), ensures the character reads clearly even at 1:1 pixel scale. Technique 2: Limited palette with maximum value contrast. Using 4-6 carefully chosen colors with strong value differences (light skin against dark armor, bright hair against muted background) ensures each body part is distinguishable. Every pixel of color boundary becomes a shape-defining edge, so high value contrast between adjacent regions is essential for readability.

rubric.key: First technique is specific to low-resolution pixel art constraints with clear reasoning
Second technique is distinct from the first and equally specific to the resolution constraint
Both techniques demonstrate understanding of why standard high-resolution approaches fail at 16x16

questions.question: In game typography, why is it important to pair a display font with a highly legible body font? Give an example of a poor pairing and explain what makes it fail.
questions.answer: Display fonts (decorative, expressive) attract attention and establish mood for titles and headers, but sacrifice legibility at small sizes or in long passages. A body font must be highly legible because players read ability descriptions, dialogue, tutorials, and item stats under time pressure. Pairing them correctly means each font serves its role without competing. Poor pairing example: using a blackletter (gothic) display font for the game title alongside a similarly decorative script font for body text. Both fonts have low legibility due to ornate letterforms, and their visual weight and complexity compete rather than contrast. The result is a UI that is difficult to read, especially on small screens, and lacks visual hierarchy because neither font clearly signals priority.

rubric.key: Explains the distinct functional roles of display vs. body fonts in game UI
Specific poor pairing example with named font categories
Explains why the pairing fails with reference to legibility, competition, and hierarchy

questions.question: Explain what boolean operations are in vector graphics and describe how you would use the 'subtract' operation to create a donut shape from two circles. Then explain why this approach is better than drawing the donut freehand.
questions.answer: Boolean operations combine two or more vector shapes using set-theory logic: union merges shapes, subtract cuts one from another, intersect keeps only the overlap, and exclude keeps only the non-overlap. To create a donut: draw a large circle (the outer ring) and a smaller circle (the inner hole), center the small circle inside the large one, then apply the subtract operation with the large circle as the base and the small circle as the cutting shape. The result is a perfect donut with a clean, mathematically exact inner hole. This is better than freehand because: (1) the curves are mathematically perfect, impossible to achieve by hand drawing; (2) the shape is infinitely scalable without quality loss; (3) the inner and outer edges are perfectly smooth; (4) modifying the shape later (changing the ring width) requires only resizing the inner circle and reapplying the boolean, not redrawing the entire shape.

rubric.key: Correct definition of boolean operations with all four types named
Step-by-step description of creating a donut using subtract that would actually work
Explains at least two advantages of boolean approach over freehand drawing

quizzes.topic: UI Design, Concept Art, and Character Design

questions.question: Explain the difference between diegetic and non-diegetic UI in games. Give one example of each from a published game and explain the advantage of each approach.
questions.answer: Diegetic UI exists within the game world as an object the character can see. Example: Dead Space's health meter is displayed on the character's spine as a glowing bar, visible to both the player and other characters in the game world. Advantage: maintains immersion because the UI is part of the world rather than an overlay that reminds the player they are playing a game. Non-diegetic UI is a traditional screen overlay that exists only for the player. Example: Overwatch's health bar, ability cooldowns, and kill feed are HUD elements the characters cannot see. Advantage: maximum readability and flexibility because elements can be positioned anywhere on screen, sized for optimal legibility, and standardized across all characters regardless of camera angle.

rubric.key: Correct definitions of diegetic and non-diegetic UI
Accurate game examples for each type with the specific UI element identified
Explains a genuine advantage of each approach with reasoning

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'shape language' in character design. A game has two characters: a friendly shopkeeper and a dangerous villain. Describe the dominant shapes you would use for each and explain why.
questions.answer: Shape language is the practice of using basic geometric shapes to communicate character personality at a subconscious level. Circles and curves suggest warmth, friendliness, and approachability. Triangles and sharp angles suggest danger, aggression, and unpredictability. Squares and rectangles suggest stability, reliability, and strength. For the friendly shopkeeper: dominant circles and rounded shapes. Round body, round face, soft curved shoulders, a round belly suggesting warmth and generosity. Circular elements in costume (round buttons, a round hat, a curved apron). Why: circles are non-threatening and inviting, signaling to the player that this character is safe to approach and interact with. For the dangerous villain: dominant triangles and sharp angles. Angular face (pointed chin, sharp cheekbones), triangular shoulders, pointed costume elements (spiked collar, angular crown, sharp-edged cape), a tall/narrow silhouette. Why: triangles evoke teeth, claws, and blades, subconsciously triggering the viewer's danger response before they read any dialogue or lore.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of shape language with the three basic shapes and their associations
Shopkeeper design uses appropriate shapes with specific visual details
Villain design uses appropriate shapes with specific visual details and contrast to the shopkeeper
