questions.question: You are creating concept art for a new creature. Describe the silhouette exploration technique and explain why an art director would prefer to see 20 rough silhouettes rather than one detailed concept drawing at this stage.
questions.answer: Silhouette exploration is the technique of drawing many small, solid-black shape studies (typically 20-30) to explore a wide range of possible designs before adding any detail. Each silhouette takes 30-60 seconds, forcing the artist to think in large shapes rather than details. An art director prefers 20 rough silhouettes because: (1) Variety: 20 silhouettes explore a vastly wider design space than one detailed drawing, increasing the chance of finding an unexpected, original concept. (2) Efficiency: if the first concept is rejected, the artist has lost 30 seconds rather than a full day of detailed work. (3) Shape-first thinking: a creature that reads in silhouette will read at any level of detail; one that only works with detail will fail at game-play distance. (4) Collaboration: the art director can circle their favorite silhouettes and provide direction before the artist invests in refinement, reducing wasted revision cycles.

rubric.key: Correct description of the silhouette exploration technique with practical details
At least two distinct reasons why 20 silhouettes beat one detailed drawing
Connects the technique to production efficiency or art direction workflow

quizzes.topic: Environment Art, Asset Pipelines, and Animation Principles

questions.question: Explain atmospheric perspective and how it creates depth in a 2D game environment. Describe three specific visual changes that occur as objects get farther from the viewer.
questions.answer: Atmospheric perspective is the visual phenomenon where the atmosphere between the viewer and distant objects scatters and filters light, causing predictable visual changes with distance. Three changes: (1) Value shift: distant objects become lighter, closer to the value of the sky, because light scattered by the atmosphere adds brightness. (2) Saturation reduction: distant objects become less saturated, appearing more gray, because atmospheric particles filter out color wavelengths. (3) Detail reduction: distant objects show less fine detail because atmospheric haze obscures edges and textures, making them softer and more uniform. In a 2D game, applying these three changes to background layers (lighter, less saturated, less detailed) while keeping the foreground dark, saturated, and detailed creates a convincing illusion of three-dimensional depth on a flat screen.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of atmospheric perspective as a physical phenomenon
Three distinct visual changes correctly described with specificity
Connects the concept to practical application in 2D game environment art

questions.question: Explain the difference between a sprite sheet and a texture atlas, and describe why game engines prefer these formats over individual image files.
questions.answer: A sprite sheet contains multiple animation frames of a single entity arranged in a grid, typically one row per animation (idle row, walk row, attack row). The engine reads frames sequentially to play animations. A texture atlas contains diverse, unrelated assets (different icons, different sprites, different UI elements) packed tightly into a single image with a metadata file describing each asset's position and dimensions. Game engines prefer both formats over individual files because of GPU draw calls. Each separate texture requires its own draw call to the GPU, which is expensive. When multiple assets share a single texture image, the engine can render them all in one draw call (batching), dramatically improving rendering performance. This matters most on mobile devices and when drawing many sprites simultaneously (hundreds of enemies, particles, UI elements).

rubric.key: Correct distinction between sprite sheet (animation frames) and texture atlas (diverse assets)
Explains the draw call / batching performance benefit correctly
Notes when the performance benefit matters most (mobile, many sprites)

questions.question: Name and explain three of Disney's 12 principles of animation that are most critical for game character animation, and for each, describe how the principle is applied differently in games versus film.
questions.answer: Principle 1: Anticipation. In film, anticipation can be long and dramatic (a character winding up for several seconds before throwing a punch). In games, anticipation must be short enough to maintain responsiveness to player input but long enough to telegraph enemy attacks for fairness. A 3-frame anticipation is typical for player attacks; 5-8 frames for enemy attacks that the player must dodge. Principle 2: Squash and Stretch. In film, squash and stretch can be extreme for comedic effect. In games, the degree must be consistent with the art style and must not interfere with collision boundaries. A character that squashes too much might visually miss a collision box, confusing the player about hit detection. Principle 3: Timing. In film, timing is set once and experienced passively. In games, timing directly affects gameplay feel: a sword swing that takes 12 frames feels fast and responsive; one that takes 24 frames feels heavy and committed. Game animation timing must be tuned in engine during gameplay, not just evaluated in the animation tool.

rubric.key: Three distinct principles correctly named and explained
Each principle's game-specific application is distinct from its film application
Demonstrates understanding of how game constraints (input responsiveness, collision, feel) change animation practice

quizzes.topic: Art Direction, Style Guides, and Professional Practice

questions.question: Explain why a style guide is essential for a game art team with more than one artist. Describe three specific sections that a style guide should contain, and for each, explain what happens if that section is missing.
questions.answer: A style guide ensures visual consistency when multiple artists contribute to the same project. Without one, each artist makes independent visual decisions, producing a game that looks like it was made by different teams. Section 1: Color palette with hex codes and usage rules. Without it, artists choose colors by eye, producing inconsistent environments (one forest is teal, another is lime green) and characters that clash with backgrounds. Section 2: Proportion system defining character scale and body ratios. Without it, characters from different artists have different head-to-body ratios, creating visual inconsistency when they appear together. Section 3: Line weight and rendering standards defining outline thickness, shading approach, and detail level. Without it, one artist produces thick-outlined cel-shaded characters while another produces thin-lined painterly characters, and the game looks like a collage rather than a cohesive world.

rubric.key: Clearly explains why consistency requires a style guide in multi-artist teams
Three distinct sections with specific content descriptions, not just section names
Each missing-section consequence is specific, plausible, and demonstrates understanding of production reality

questions.question: You are reviewing a junior artist's portfolio for a game art position. The portfolio has 20 pieces of varying quality. What advice would you give about curation, and what would you look for beyond technical skill?
questions.answer: Curation advice: reduce to the 8-10 strongest pieces. A portfolio is only as strong as its weakest piece, and including mediocre work alongside excellent work averages down the overall impression. For each remaining piece, include process work (thumbnails, iterations, final) because it demonstrates design thinking. If the portfolio covers multiple disciplines (character, environment, UI), group pieces by type so the reviewer can evaluate depth in each area. Beyond technical skill, I would look for: (1) Design thinking: can the artist explain why they made specific visual choices, not just how? This separates an artist who can follow direction from one who can make direction. (2) Problem-solving evidence: does the work show solutions to real game art problems (readability at small size, consistent style, functional UI layout), not just pretty illustrations? (3) Growth trajectory: does comparing earlier and later pieces show learning and improvement? A junior artist who improves quickly is more valuable than one with higher current skill but no growth pattern.

rubric.key: Clear curation advice with specific quantity and quality reasoning
At least two beyond-technical-skill criteria that are relevant to game art hiring
Includes practical portfolio presentation advice (process work, grouping, format)

questions.question: A game is being developed for both mobile (phone) and console (TV viewed from couch). Identify two specific art and UI decisions that must change between platforms and explain why.
questions.answer: Decision 1: UI text size and touch target dimensions. On mobile, minimum text size is 16px and interactive elements need 44x44 point touch targets because the input device (fingers) is imprecise. On console, minimum text size is 24px because the screen is viewed from 6-10 feet, but interactive elements can be smaller because gamepad navigation moves between elements precisely. The same 14px font that is readable on a phone held 18 inches away becomes unreadable on a TV 8 feet away. Decision 2: HUD density and screen composition. Mobile screens are small and every pixel is visible, so HUDs can be information-dense with small indicators in corners. Console screens are large but viewed from distance, so HUD elements need to be larger, fewer in number, and more spread out. Additionally, console TVs have overscan that can clip content near edges, requiring a 10% safe zone margin that mobile does not need.

rubric.key: Two distinct decisions that genuinely differ between platforms
Specific technical details for each platform (sizes, distances, margins)
Explains the underlying reason for each difference (viewing distance, input method, screen characteristics)

major_assignments.description: Create a comprehensive art style guide for an original game concept, then produce a complete set of game-ready assets that consistently follow the style guide. The asset set must include character art with animation, environment tiles, UI elements, and icons, demonstrating mastery of multiple game art disciplines and a professional asset pipeline.
objectives: Define and document a cohesive visual style that can guide a multi-artist team

deliverables: Style guide document (5-7 pages) covering color palette with hex codes, typography selections with usage rules, proportion system, line weight standards, rendering approach, and do/don't examples

major_assignments.title: Animated Game Scene
major_assignments.description: Create a fully animated game scene combining environment art, a playable character with a complete animation set, and UI elements. The scene must demonstrate mastery of the 12 animation principles, asset pipeline skills, and visual composition. All assets must follow a consistent art style and be assembled in a game engine or animation tool for final presentation.

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is required for all sessions. Studio time, live demonstrations, and critique sessions cannot be replicated outside class. More than 3 unexcused absences will reduce the final grade by one half-letter.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work is penalized 10% per day. Critique deadlines are firm because peer review depends on all work being available simultaneously. The final portfolio presentation date cannot be rescheduled.
course_policies.collaboration: Artistic inspiration from others is natural, but all submitted work must be your own creation. Tracing or copying another artist's work without explicit permission and disclosure constitutes academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted art must be original. Use of AI image generators for submitted work is prohibited. Reference images must be cited. Stock assets may not be submitted as original work.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need access to a graphics tablet with pressure sensitivity and at least one raster editor (Photoshop, Krita, or Clip Studio Paint), one vector editor (Illustrator, Inkscape, or Figma), and one pixel art editor (Aseprite or Piskel). Lab computers with all software are available during open studio hours.
